lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators in NAICS 459510 that repeatedly perform managed resale or consignment for external sellers, including intake, valuation, merchandising, sale, fulfillment, and seller settlement.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure inventory-owning used-goods stores, one-off dealers, charities without transferable commercial operations, owner-hobby antique shops, software-only marketplaces, pawnshops, motor-vehicle dealers, and firms outside the roughly $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The qualifying model earns repeat consignment commissions, seller fees, or a managed-resale spread from households, estates, brands, and institutions while also completing retail transactions with end buyers.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is heterogeneous between ordinary goods retail and managed resale/consignment. The lens retains only firms whose repeated intake-to-sale workflow constitutes an outsourced service to external sellers; ordinary inventory-owning retailers are excluded because they do not satisfy the fixed service lens.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for combined NAICS 4594 and 4595, not 459510 or the managed-resale lens. I mapped its wage-weighted occupation mix to exposed workflows: product-description and marketing generation, customer support, seller communications, bookkeeping, inventory records, search, and pricing are exposed, while physical intake, inspection, authentication, stocking, and face-to-face selling are largely not. Combining adjacent industries likely understates exposure if managed resale is more digital than gift and office-supply retail, while task-level judgment may overstate substitution where item uniqueness requires human review.
a.rationale: BLS reports 61.34% of employment in sales occupations, 9.27% in office and administrative support, 11.22% in material moving, and 6.49% in management for the adjacent-industry grouping. Most wage mass remains in physical or interpersonal work, but listing creation, support, recordkeeping, demand matching, and pricing create a meaningful exposed slice. Anthropic finds customer service and data entry among the most exposed occupations while emphasizing that real-world coverage remains below theoretical capability.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary from 20-30 managed consignment and resale operators, linked to occupation wages and separating listing, pricing, support, intake, inspection, fulfillment, and store-floor time, would materially move this estimate.
caveats: The BLS industry group combines NAICS 4594 and 4595 and excludes self-employed workers.
The task mapping is judgmental and measures technical exposure, not realized five-year implementation.

The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio is LOW quality, measured at ancestor 44-45, uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, and is harmonized to the IPS basis; it may not represent managed resale labor intensity.

rho.rationale: Within the exposed opportunity, five-year implementation should be substantial for drafting, support triage, image enrichment, search, and pricing recommendations. Full substitution is constrained by fragmented point-of-sale systems, small-firm change capacity, poor item data, authenticity risk, exception handling, and the need for employees to approve unique-item pricing and condition claims.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hour and headcount data from deployments of AI listing, pricing, and customer-service tools at at least ten multi-location resale operators, including exception rates and human-review time, would materially change the range.
caveats: ThredUp is a large technology-led apparel operator and may implement faster than independent LMM firms.
No source measures implemented substitution for the qualifying six-digit population.

e.rationale: Census defines the industry around retailing used goods, not supplying outsourced services. Consignment shops and managed resale operations are included, but ordinary thrift, antique, book, appliance, sporting-goods, and inventory-owning secondhand stores dominate the code conceptually. Only the minority with repeat seller-side intake, sale, and settlement service fits the fixed lens.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or commercial-business database coded by revenue model, nonprofit status, EBITDA band, and consignment or managed-resale share would materially change eligibility.
caveats: There is no observed share of LMM firms using a managed-resale or consignment model.
The finalizer will inject MISSING for the declared dataset gap in n, so the absolute eligible-firm count is unavailable.

s5.rationale: Independent retail businesses do transact, and BizBuySell reports a national market of mostly independent, locally owned retail businesses. A five-year control-transfer probability near one in five allows for owner retirements and ordinary sale flow while excluding closures, asset-only liquidations, internal reorganizations, and firms whose seller relationships or sourcing do not transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-based five-year cohort from BizBuySell, DealStats, GF Data, or Axial matched to NAICS 459510, EBITDA band, and confirmed control changes would materially change this probability.
caveats: BizBuySell reports sold-business characteristics rather than a transfer rate against the eligible-firm population.
Small retail transaction data skew toward much smaller firms: the five-year median sold-business owner earnings were $131,498, far below the lens EBITDA floor.

q.rationale: Managed resale prices or commissions are set per transaction rather than contractually cost-plus, so early productivity gains can remain with the operator. Over five years, sellers can demand better payouts and buyers can compare prices across local and online channels, passing part of the benefit through. Unique inventory, proprietary supply, convenience, and trust slow complete repricing.
q.change_evidence: Consignment agreements, seller payout schedules, gross-margin cohorts, and price-response data before and after automation from multiple operators would show how much benefit is shared with sellers and buyers.
caveats: The code spans commission, donated-inventory, auction, and owned-inventory models with different pass-through mechanics.
Competitive response over five years is not directly observed.

d5.bridge: GlobalData's ThredUp report projects U.S. secondhand apparel to $78.8 billion by 2030 and describes the current-to-2030 increment as $23.3 billion, implying strong nominal apparel-resale growth. I use only part of that growth after allowing for inflation and weaker or declining nonapparel categories such as books and media. Apparel is a subset of NAICS 459510 and the source includes online resale beyond LMM operators, so it likely overstates growth for store-heavy general used merchandise; omitting reuse tailwinds in furniture, equipment, and collectibles could bias the adjustment downward.
d5.rationale: Affordability, younger-consumer adoption, brand participation, and improved digital discovery support real demand growth, but the whole code is broader and slower than apparel resale. The range allows for macroeconomic normalization, category substitution, and online platforms taking volume from operator-run stores.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted Census sales or card-spend panels for NAICS 459510 by merchandise category and channel through 2031 would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The source is commissioned by a major apparel resale platform and is not a whole-industry forecast.
The published forecast is market value, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity.
The projection ends in 2030, one year before the five-year horizon from 2026.

o.rationale: Software can self-serve discovery, listing assistance, and some peer-to-peer exchange, but the qualifying managed-resale service still needs an accountable operator for custody, inspection, condition grading, authentication, returns, dispute resolution, seller settlement, and physical fulfillment. The operator-required share falls where platforms let sellers list and ship directly or brands internalize resale.
o.change_evidence: A five-year channel-shift study measuring GMV moving among managed consignment, peer-to-peer, brand-owned resale, and pure software marketplaces, plus operator-touch rates by transaction, would materially move this value.
caveats: Operator requirement differs sharply by category: luxury authentication and bulky goods need more intervention than books or standardized apparel.
The estimate concerns quantity still requiring an accountable qualifying operator, not whether individual tasks remain manual.

narrative.executive_view: The qualifying opportunity is much narrower than the NAICS label: most used-merchandise businesses sell goods rather than provide an outsourced service. Managed consignment and resale operators do have repeat seller workflows, growing demand in important categories, and automatable digital work, but physical item handling and trust remain central.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft listings from images and notes, enrich attributes, recommend prices, match inventory to demand, answer routine buyer and seller questions, localize marketing, and automate bookkeeping and settlement exceptions. It is less able to remove intake, cleaning, condition grading, authentication, display, packing, pickup, and relationship-heavy sourcing.
narrative.value_capture: Transaction pricing gives an operator room to retain initial labor savings, especially when convenience, curation, supply access, and trust differentiate the service. Retention erodes as sellers compare payout rates, buyers compare identical or similar goods across platforms, and competitors deploy the same tools.
narrative.firm_availability: A visible market exists for independent retail-business sales, but most observed transactions are far below the stated EBITDA band. The harder constraint is eligibility: only managed resale or consignment models meet the outsourced-service lens, and no denominator-based count or transfer rate exists for that subset.
narrative.demand_durability: Secondhand apparel research indicates strong category growth and wider consumer adoption, while affordability and sustainability support reuse more broadly. The current service basket will still need custody, inspection, fulfillment, and accountability, though peer-to-peer platforms and brand-owned programs can bypass independent operators.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal uncertainties are the tiny and unmeasured eligible share, the missing LMM firm-count input, broad proxy occupation data, and a category-biased demand forecast. Inventory uniqueness limits standardization, fraud and authenticity errors can create losses, and online marketplaces can capture both sellers and buyers.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat, data-rich listing, pricing, support, and settlement workflows can be improved while managed resale demand continues to expand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Only a small, unmeasured subset of used-merchandise retailers fits the recurring outsourced-service lens, and the eligible LMM firm count is missing.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 459510 — Used Merchandise Retailers
sources.supports: Official industry boundary: used merchandise, antiques, secondhand goods, and used-merchandise auctions; illustrative and cross-reference detail includes consignment shops and exclusions.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS — Miscellaneous Store Retailers (4594 and 4595 only)
sources.supports: Observed adjacent-industry occupation mix and wages, including 61.34% sales, 9.27% office and administrative support, 11.22% material moving, and 6.49% management employment shares.

sources.title: Labor market impacts of AI: A new measure and early evidence
sources.supports: Task-based theoretical and observed AI exposure methodology; customer service and data entry are among highly covered occupations; actual coverage remains below theoretical capability and physical tasks remain beyond reach.

sources.title: ThredUp's 14th Annual Resale Report
sources.supports: GlobalData-derived U.S. apparel resale forecast to $78.8 billion by 2030, $23.3 billion incremental value, survey methodology, social discovery, AI use in pricing and authentication, and managed resale operating context.

sources.title: Retail Business Valuation Benchmarks
sources.supports: Reported retail business transaction and financial benchmarks for 2021-2025, including mostly independent local ownership and median sold-business owner earnings of $131,498.
